features: The extracted requirements are mapped to corresponding system features. This mapping establishes a clear relationship between requirements and potential implementation components, supporting traceability and design alignment.

All generated data—including document metadata, sections, extracted requirements, and mapped features—are persistently stored in an SQLite database. This ensures data consistency, reliability, and accessibility for downstream processes such as querying, analysis, or integration with other systems.

The present task offers a successful illustration of how to split software requirements and extract software features in a software development environment using an automated, scalable, and methodical approach. The platform provides the foundation for intelligent systems to be taught on these needs and utilised for upcoming AI-based requirement analysis by connecting unstructured requirements documents with structured knowledge bases.
clean_text: task overview problem statement the unstructured execution of a standard software requirement specification (srs) document makes its automatic analysis extremely difficult. when all of the srs documents are integrated, this method of requirement analysis is time-consuming, error-prone, and does not support scale. objective the objective of this task is to design and implement an automated pipeline that: extracts text from srs pdfs segments srs documents into logical sections extracts functional requirements identifies software features stores all extracted knowledge in a structured database system scope in scope pdf-based srs documents functional and non-functional requirement extraction feature categorization multi-document storage using sqlite out of scope requirement validation requirement prioritization full llm-based reasoning (future work) system architecture technology stack table 1: technology stack database design database schema overview documents table 2:document table sections table 3: sections table requirements table 4: requirements table features table 5: feature table text extraction module input: srs pdf output: raw extracted text purpose: converts unstructured pdf into machine-readable text srs section segmentation module identifies standard srs headings (introduction, functional requirements, etc.) uses regex-based pattern matching saves segmented sections into database requirement extraction module tokenizes functional requirements extracts sentences following ieee srs standards filters requirement-like statements (“shall”) feature extraction module maps requirements to software features uses rule-based keyword classification supports one-to-many mapping database integration module stores extracted knowledge persistently enables querying and reuse prevents output folder overload end to end pipeline execution the complete srs processing pipeline is executed using a single command: “python src/main.py” this command initiates an automated end-to-end workflow designed to process software requirement specification (srs) documents efficiently and reliably. upon execution, the system performs the following sequential operations: document registration the uploaded srs document is first registered within the system. metadata such as the document name and a unique document identifier are generated and stored in the database. this ensures traceability and enables future retrieval or reprocessing of the document. section segmentation and storage the content of the srs document is extracted and segmented into logical sections based on standard srs structure. each identified section is then stored in the database, preserving the original document hierarchy for structured analysis. requirement extraction the segmented sections are analyzed to automatically identify and extract functional and non-functional requirements. this step transforms unstructured textual content into structured requirement entities that can be further processed. feature mapping the extracted requirements are mapped to corresponding system features. this mapping establishes a clear relationship between requirements and potential implementation components, supporting traceability and design alignment. data persistence all generated data—including document metadata, sections, extracted requirements, and mapped features—are persistently stored in an sqlite database. this ensures data consistency, reliability, and accessibility for downstream processes such as querying, analysis, or integration with other systems. overall, this single-command execution demonstrates a fully automated and scalable pipeline that converts raw srs documents into structured, actionable data, reducing manual effort and improving accuracy in requirement analysis. results and observations successfully extracted 18 functional requirements generated 62 feature mappings system supports multiple srs documents database ensures scalability and traceability figure 1: sqlite_sequence limitations rule-based feature extraction no semantic similarity matching yet language-specific (english srs) future enhancements this section is gold for mentors 👇 integrate llm + rag for intelligent querying use vector embeddings for semantic search add requirement prioritization support multiple document formats build a dashboard for analytics conclusion the present task offers a successful illustration of how to split software requirements and extract software features in a software development environment using an automated, scalable, and methodical approach. the platform provides the foundation for intelligent systems to be taught on these needs and utilised for upcoming ai-based requirement analysis by connecting unstructured requirements documents with structured knowledge bases. figure 2: database successfully initialized figure 3: created database in sqlite figure 4: document inserted into the database figure 5: evidence of document being inserted into the database figure 6: srs section being inserted figure 7: srs sections in database figure 8: requirements inserted figure 9: 18 requirements being inserted figure 10: feature mappings inserted figure 11: 31 features being inserted
